shower.thought: We spend our whole lives trying to find out who we are, but the only person who can't see us is ourselves.
Your worst fear about what people think of you is probably something you thought of them first.
The people you were closest to in third grade are now complete strangers, and you can't even pinpoint when that happened.
Success tastes different depending on whether you're running toward something or away from something.
Most of your life is spent in rooms you'll never think about again.
You'll never know if you quit something too early or stayed too long—only your regrets will decide.
The moment you stop needing someone's approval is usually the moment you finally get it.
You can't miss something you never had, but somehow you do anyway.
Your life would be completely different if one person had said yes instead of no, and you'll never know which person that was.
We memorize the words people say but forget the way they made us feel, then years later a song brings it all back.
The people closest to you have no idea about the internal conversations you have with them every day.
You're probably someone's cautionary tale without even knowing it.
Every version of yourself that you killed to become who you are now is still haunting you a little.
The saddest part about getting older isn't losing time—it's realizing how much of it you spent waiting.
You won't remember most of today, but today is becoming the person you'll be tomorrow.
Loyalty to people who've already moved on is just another kind of being alone.
The things you regret most aren't usually the risks you took—they're the people you didn't text back.
You're never too old to start something new, but you're getting older every single day you don't.
Pain makes people interesting; comfort makes them forgettable.
Some people have known you your entire life but don't actually know you at all.

motivational.thought: The obstacle in the path becomes the path. — Marcus Aurelius
You are not behind. You are not late. You are exactly where you need to be to become who you're meant to be. — Unknown
Comparison is the thief of progress. — Unknown
The greatest regret in life is not for the things you did, but for the things you didn't try. — Unknown
Your excuses are just reasons you haven't found your why yet. — Unknown
Everything you want is on the other side of fear. — George Addair
The only person you should try to be better than is the person you were yesterday. — Unknown
Success is not final, failure is not fatal. It is the courage to continue that counts. — Winston Churchill
You don't rise to the level of your goals, you fall to the level of your systems. — James Clear
Stop waiting for the right time. The right time is now. — Unknown
The pain of discipline weighs ounces while the pain of regret weighs tons. — Jim Rohn
Your worth is not measured by your productivity. Your value is inherent. Now go build something anyway. — Unknown
What seems impossible today will seem obvious tomorrow. You're just ahead of schedule. — Unknown
The person who moves a mountain begins by carrying away small stones. — Confucius
You don't need motivation. You need to build a life where you don't need it. — Unknown
A man who conquers himself is greater than one who conquers a thousand men in battle. — Buddha
The quiet voice that tells you to try is more important than the loud voice that tells you it's impossible. — Unknown
Your future is not predetermined. It's determined by what you do in this moment. — Unknown
Progress is not about giant leaps. It's about the small steps you repeat when no one is watching. — Unknown
I am not what happened to me. I am what I choose to become. — Carl Jung

darkjoke.thought: My therapist asked why I keep sabotaging my own happiness. I said, "At least I'm consistent at something."
I told my friend I'm reading a book on suicide prevention. He asked what it's about. I said, "Honestly, I have no idea—I keep putting it down."
My doctor said I need to cut back on drinking. I told him I'd try, but my liver's been my only friend for three years.
I went to a support group for people with low self-esteem. Turns out I was too good for it.
My mortgage broker asked where I see myself in five years. I said, "Probably still in this house, but angrier."
I told my cat I was having an existential crisis. She knocked my coffee off the table. Finally, someone understands.
My gym membership is like a relationship—I pay for it monthly and never show up, but I feel guilty canceling.
I asked a friend if he thought I was depressing to be around. He said, "No, but let me finish my drink first."
My resume says I'm a "self-starter." What it means is nobody told me to stop.
I tried to befriend my sadness so we could work through it together. Turns out we're pretty close already.
My divorce lawyer said, "This is going to be messy." I said, "Good—at least something in my life will be intentional."
I told my accountant I wanted to turn my life around. He said, "Even I can't help with that—I just count money."
My insomnia and I have been together longer than most marriages. At this point we're basically common-law married.
I asked my therapist if she thinks I'll ever get better. She checked her watch. We had 40 minutes left.
My mother asked when I'm getting married. I said, "When I find someone with low enough standards." She said, "Good luck."
I went to buy self-help books and realized I couldn't afford to help myself.
My Spotify wrapped said my top genre was "sad indie folk." My second genre was "my life choices."
I told my boss I was having a mental health day. He said, "You're having a mental health life. That's why we pay you so little."
I tried to write a suicide note once but realized nobody reads anything I write anyway.
My dreams are like my bank account—unfulfilled and slightly embarrassing to check.
